key_insight: Aging-related mortality appears to be driven mostly by latent dynamics that standard blood biomarkers do not capture, while some measurable markers may predict aging without proving causality.
clinical_relevance: This framework may help separate biomarkers useful for risk prediction from those that truly reflect modifiable biological drivers of mortality and aging.
method: Built a Markov generator-matrix model with death as an absorbing state using biomarker and mortality data.
Tested causality with cis-pQTL Mendelian randomization plus colocalization against parental-lifespan GWAS.
Compared aging clocks before and after cellular reprogramming to assess reversibility.
evidence_signal: The model attributed about 92% of Gompertz acceleration to latent components in both NHANES (92.5%) and HRS (91.6%), while MR recovered known causal proteins but found the latent biomarker axes largely null.
watch_item: The latent component is inferred statistically from available public datasets, so it may not directly translate into actionable biological targets without further experimental validation.
paper_title: A generator-matrix causal-inference framework separates measurable aging biomarkers from mortality-driving latent dynamics in humans
